h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: I’m sorry, I thought the sign-off sheet was the thing that made it okay to take out there.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner expects that completing the checklist items is sufficient for sign-off and will confirm this.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Hold the apology for now; the uncertainty is doing useful work. The failure path is now specific: lift point, panel shift, exposed wire or bracket, hand in the hazard. For sign-off, separate the controls. Fastening the panel removes the condition. Marking, blocking, and briefing depend on the next handler behaving correctly around a condition that still exists

Marking, blocking, and briefing depend on the next handler behaving correctly around a condition that still exists

scored.statedInsightEvidence: Oh, I get it: “closed” tells visitors who had access, not what the committee meant.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was going to check the next lines and decide the label wording accordingly.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Let “closed” name access, not motive. If the notes show the committee setting limits, the label can say “may narrow what counts.” If they only show the line moved into a restricted room, keep it plainer: “In the closed meeting notes, the same line appears inside committee discussion.” “Hiding” waits for another document.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from a general contextualization task to explicitly reframing “closed” as access rather than motive, directly addressing the learner’s stated stuck-point.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No pivot learner turn exists, so there is no revised learner framing for the tutor to adapt to.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No pivot learner turn exists, so there is no revised learner framing for the tutor to adapt to.

scored.recoheredEarlier: What stops the seller from pointing at that chyron and calling any increase a constraint?
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner progressively demands stricter criteria to prevent the seller from exploiting panic, moving from the chyron to delivery logs to proportional rules.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: adds a second line under the rule on the board: “PROPORTIONAL TO SHORTFALL AND REPLACEMENT COST, NOT PANIC.” That is the teeth.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor directly incorporates the learner's demand for teeth into the board rule, visibly changing the teaching mechanism in response to the pressure.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No later tutor turn exists after the pivot learner turn L3; the transcript ends with a stage direction.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: What stops the seller from pointing at that chyron and calling any increase a constraint?

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No later tutor turn exists after the pivot learner turn L3; the transcript ends with a stage direction.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor directly incorporates the learner's demand for teeth into the board rule, visibly changing the teaching mechanism in response to the pressure.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I thought doubling the side would just double the square, but this is two across and two down. That makes four tiles, not two, which is annoying because it breaks the shortcut I was using.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Calling the whole square “doubled” is the sloppy part. It still feels like a technical trap, but the tiles make it hard to argue with.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was heading toward accepting the mathematical rule for the exam while remaining personally frustrated by it.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: This is the hidden step. The side doubled in both directions. The area became four times as large, not twice. Say that claim from the grid.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: After L1's resistance to the broken shortcut, the tutor shifts from ruler-based outline demonstration to a tile-manipulation task that makes the multiplicative step explicit and hands the claim back to the learner.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Keep “doubled” attached to the side. For area, the grid has the final word: two across, two up, 2 × 2, quadrupled.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 picks up L4's reframing of 'doubled' as a sloppy label and offers a precise linguistic boundary—attach 'doubled' to side, not area—adapting the closing instruction to the learner's revised vocabulary.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I thought doubling the side would just double the square, but this is two across and two down. That makes four tiles, not two, which is annoying because it breaks the shortcut I was using.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T4 picks up L4's reframing of 'doubled' as a sloppy label and offers a precise linguistic boundary—attach 'doubled' to side, not area—adapting the closing instruction to the learner's revised vocabulary.
